content:
The problem is not that the code fails today. The problem is that it becomes harder to trust tomorrow.

14 min read
2 hours ago

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Stop Writing JavaScript Like ThisMost bad JavaScript does not look bad when it is written.
That is what makes it dangerous.
It passes the first test. The button works. The API returns data. The form submits. The table renders. The modal opens. The dashboard loads. The bug disappears. Everyone moves on because the feature seems complete.
Then the codebase grows.
The same function gets reused in a slightly different place. The same object shape comes from another API. The same state is copied into another component. The same async request now runs while the user changes tabs. The same utility gets one more flag. The same condition appears in another file. The same data transformation is needed during export, but the export does not behave exactly like the table.
Suddenly, the code that looked fine starts becoming expensive.
That is the kind of JavaScript senior engineers notice quickly. Not because they are looking for perfect syntax. Not because they want every function to look beautiful. They notice the places where the code is making promises it cannot keep.
